title: How to Build a DIY Compost Bin on a Budget
excerpt: You do not need an expensive setup to compost effectively at home. A simple DIY bin made from wire mesh, pallets, a plastic tote, or a modified trash can can provide all the basics needed to turn everyday waste into useful compost.
author: Permatable Team
content:
DIY Composting

You do not need an expensive setup to start composting at home. A simple DIY compost bin can work just as well as many store-bought options, especially for beginners. If your goal is to turn kitchen scraps and yard waste into useful compost without spending much money, building your own bin is a practical place to start.

The best part is that a budget compost bin can be made from basic materials you may already have at home or can find cheaply. With a little planning, you can create a bin that is simple, functional, and easy to maintain.

Why Build Your Own Compost Bin?

A homemade compost bin can save money, but that is not the only reason people choose one.

A DIY compost bin also lets you:

  • Control the size
  • Use recycled materials
  • Match the bin to your available space
  • Keep the setup simple
  • Start composting right away

For many gardeners, a basic homemade bin is more than enough to make good compost.

What a Good Budget Compost Bin Needs

A compost bin does not have to be fancy, but it does need a few important features.

A good compost bin should:

  • Hold materials in one place
  • Allow some airflow
  • Let excess moisture drain
  • Be easy to access
  • Fit the amount of compost you expect to make

As long as your bin does these things reasonably well, it can work.

The Cheapest DIY Compost Bin Ideas

There are several low-cost ways to build a compost bin.

1. Wire Mesh Bin

One of the easiest and cheapest options is a wire mesh bin. This is often made from hardware cloth or garden wire formed into a circle or square.

Why it works:

  • Inexpensive
  • Easy to assemble
  • Good airflow
  • Useful for leaves and yard waste

This is a great option if you want a simple outdoor compost bin with very little construction.

2. Wooden Pallet Bin

Old pallets can be turned into a sturdy compost bin with little cost if you can source them cheaply or free.

Why it works:

  • Strong and roomy
  • Good for backyard composting
  • Uses reclaimed materials
  • Easy to customize

This is a popular option for people who want a larger bin.

3. Plastic Storage Bin

A large plastic tote can become a compact compost bin with a few drainage and airflow holes.

Why it works:

  • Low-cost
  • Tidy appearance
  • Useful for small spaces
  • Easy to move

This works better for smaller composting setups, especially if you produce limited amounts of waste.

4. Trash Can Compost Bin

A plastic trash can with holes drilled into the sides and bottom can also be used as a compost bin.

Why it works:

  • Enclosed and neat
  • Good for small yards
  • Easy to find
  • Helps keep materials contained

This option suits beginners who want a cleaner-looking bin.

Best Budget Option for Most Beginners

For many people, the best low-cost DIY compost bin is either:

  • A wire mesh bin for the backyard
  • Or a plastic bin for small spaces

The wire mesh version is usually the cheapest and easiest to build if you have room outdoors. A plastic bin is often better if you want something compact and tidy.

How to Build a Simple Wire Mesh Compost Bin

This is one of the easiest budget-friendly compost bins to make.

What You Need

  • Wire mesh or hardware cloth
  • Wire ties, zip ties, or strong garden ties
  • Gloves
  • Optional stakes for support

How to Build It

  1. Cut a section of wire mesh long enough to form a circle.
  2. Stand it upright in the area where you want the bin.
  3. Connect the ends securely with ties.
  4. If needed, add stakes to hold it in place.
  5. Start adding browns and greens inside.

This creates a basic compost enclosure that is cheap, breathable, and easy to use.

How to Build a Plastic Bin Compost System

A plastic tote or trash can can be turned into a budget compost bin in a short time.

What You Need

  • A large plastic tote or trash can with lid
  • A drill or sharp tool for making holes
  • A tray or bricks if needed for drainage support

How to Build It

  1. Drill small air holes along the sides.
  2. Add drainage holes in the bottom.
  3. Place the bin on bricks, soil, or a tray where excess moisture can escape.
  4. Add a base layer of browns like shredded cardboard or dry leaves.
  5. Begin composting with a balanced mix of greens and browns.

This type of bin is useful if you want to keep the compost more contained.

Where to Place Your DIY Compost Bin

The location matters almost as much as the bin itself.

Try to place your compost bin somewhere that is:

  • Easy to reach
  • Level
  • Well-drained
  • Not too far from your kitchen or garden
  • Partly shaded if possible

If the bin is easy to access, you are much more likely to use it regularly.

Materials You Can Use to Save Even More Money

A budget compost bin is often easiest to build from reused or low-cost materials.

Useful materials may include:

  • Old pallets
  • Leftover wire fencing
  • Plastic totes
  • Trash cans
  • Scrap wood
  • Bricks
  • Zip ties
  • Untreated wood offcuts

Using materials you already have makes the project even more affordable.

What Size Should a DIY Compost Bin Be?

The right size depends on how much waste you produce, but for many backyard compost systems, a bin around 3 feet wide by 3 feet high works well.

This size is large enough to:

  • Hold a useful volume of material
  • Retain some heat
  • Stay active more easily
  • Still remain manageable

Smaller bins can work too, but compost may break down more slowly in very tiny systems.

How to Keep a Budget Bin Working Well

A low-cost compost bin can work just as well as a more expensive one if you manage it properly.

Key habits include:

  • Adding both greens and browns
  • Keeping the pile moist but not soggy
  • Turning it occasionally
  • Avoiding meat, dairy, and oily foods
  • Covering food scraps with browns

The composting process matters more than the price of the bin.

Common Budget Bin Problems to Avoid

A DIY bin does not need to be perfect, but a few problems can make it less effective.

  • Poor airflow: bins that are too enclosed may compost slowly or smell bad
  • No drainage: if water cannot escape, the compost may become soggy
  • Bin too small: a very tiny bin may not hold enough mass for efficient composting
  • Difficult access: if the bin is awkward to open or empty, it becomes harder to maintain

Simple design choices can prevent most of these issues.

Is a Homemade Bin as Good as a Store-Bought One?

In many cases, yes. A homemade compost bin can work just as well as a commercial one, especially for basic backyard composting.

Store-bought bins may offer:

  • A tidier appearance
  • Added convenience
  • Better pest protection
  • Easier turning in some cases

But a simple DIY bin can still make excellent compost when used correctly.

Final Thoughts

Building a DIY compost bin on a budget is one of the easiest ways to start composting without spending much money. A wire mesh bin, pallet bin, plastic tote, or modified trash can can all work well when they provide airflow, drainage, and easy access.

You do not need an expensive compost system to succeed. With basic materials and a simple design, you can create a practical bin that turns everyday waste into valuable compost for your garden.
